questions.question: What is a VLAN?
options: A logical Layer 2 segmentation of a physical LAN where the switch treats each VLAN as a separate broadcast domain
A physical cable that connects two switches
A Layer 3 routing protocol for inter-site traffic
A security feature that encrypts frames on a trunk
questions.explanation: A VLAN (Virtual LAN) is a logical Layer 2 segmentation of a physical LAN; the switch treats each VLAN as a separate broadcast domain and won't forward traffic between VLANs.

questions.question: What is the default VLAN on a Cisco switch?
questions.explanation: VLAN 1 is the default VLAN; all switch interfaces are assigned to it by default.

questions.question: Which command assigns an interface to VLAN 10 as an access port member?
questions.explanation: `switchport access vlan 10` assigns the port to VLAN 10 (and auto-creates the VLAN if absent).

questions.question: What is an access port?
options: A switchport belonging to a single VLAN, usually connected to an end host
A switchport that carries traffic from multiple VLANs
A routed Layer 3 port on a multilayer switch
A console port for CLI management
questions.explanation: An access port is a switchport belonging to a single VLAN, usually connected to an end host like a PC.

questions.question: Which device is required to forward traffic between VLANs?
questions.explanation: Inter-VLAN routing is handled by a router or Layer 3 switch. Switches by themselves do not inter-VLAN route.

questions.question: What does LAN stand for?
questions.explanation: LAN stands for Local Area Network — a single broadcast domain in one location.

questions.question: What is the normal VLAN range that works on all Cisco switches?
questions.explanation: The normal VLAN range is 1-1005 and can be used on all Cisco switches. The extended range is 1006-4094.

questions.question: What is flooding in switch behavior?
options: Forwarding a frame out all interfaces except the arrival interface
Dropping all frames with unknown destinations
questions.explanation: Flooding is the switch behavior of forwarding a frame out all interfaces except the arrival interface; used for broadcasts and unknown unicast frames.

questions.question: What is the destination MAC of a broadcast frame?
questions.explanation: A broadcast frame has destination MAC all Fs (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) and is flooded by a switch out every port except the one it arrived on.

questions.question: Which command displays VLANs on the switch along with their assigned access ports?
questions.explanation: `show vlan brief` lists VLANs on the switch and which access ports are assigned to each.

questions.question: Which VLANs are reserved and cannot be deleted?
questions.explanation: VLANs 0 and 4095 can't be used; VLANs 1 and 1002-1005 exist by default and can't be deleted.

questions.question: What is an unknown unicast frame?
options: A unicast frame whose destination MAC is not in the switch's MAC table
A frame with no source MAC address
A frame that failed a CRC check
questions.explanation: An unknown unicast frame is a unicast frame whose destination MAC is not in the switch's MAC table; it is also flooded.
